verses.text: As snow in summer is unfitting, so honour is not seemly for a fool; an undeserved curse does not land; 'a whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool's back.' Fools need correction, not honour. Empty curses cannot harm the innocent.

As snow in summer is unfitting, so honour is not seemly for a fool; an undeserved curse does not land; 'a whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool's back.' Fools need correction, not honour. Empty curses cannot harm the innocent.

As snow in summer is unfitting, so honour is not seemly for a fool; an undeserved curse does not land; 'a whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool's back.' Fools need correction, not honour. Empty curses cannot harm the innocent.

Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest you be like him; yet answer a fool in his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit; sending a message by a fool is self-injury. Wisdom knows when to reply. A fool entrusted with a task brings only harm.

Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest you be like him; yet answer a fool in his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit; sending a message by a fool is self-injury. Wisdom knows when to reply. A fool entrusted with a task brings only harm.

Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest you be like him; yet answer a fool in his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit; sending a message by a fool is self-injury. Wisdom knows when to reply. A fool entrusted with a task brings only harm.

A parable in a fool's mouth is as useless as the legs of the lame; giving honour to a fool is like binding a stone in a sling; a parable in a fool's mouth is like a thorn in a drunkard's hand. Wisdom is wasted on fools. They misuse the very truths they handle.

The great God who formed all rewards both fool and transgressor; 'as a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly'; 'seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.' Folly repeats itself. Self-conceit is worse than open folly.

The great God who formed all rewards both fool and transgressor; 'as a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly'; 'seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.' Folly repeats itself. Self-conceit is worse than open folly.

The great God who formed all rewards both fool and transgressor; 'as a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly'; 'seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.' Folly repeats itself. Self-conceit is worse than open folly.

The sluggard pleads a lion in the way; he turns on his bed as a door on its hinges; he hides his hand in his dish, too lazy to eat; yet he thinks himself wiser than seven sensible men. Laziness invents excuses. The slothful are blind to their own folly.

He that meddles in a quarrel not his own grabs a dog by the ears; a deceiver who later says, 'Was I not in sport?' is as a madman casting firebrands and death. Mind your own strife. Reckless 'joking' that harms others is no joke at all.

Without wood the fire goes out, and without a talebearer strife ceases; as coals to burning, so a contentious man kindles strife; 'the words of a talebearer are as wounds.' Gossip fuels conflict. The slanderer's words sink deep and divide.

Without wood the fire goes out, and without a talebearer strife ceases; as coals to burning, so a contentious man kindles strife; 'the words of a talebearer are as wounds.' Gossip fuels conflict. The slanderer's words sink deep and divide.

Burning lips over a wicked heart are like a clay pot coated with silver dross; the hater disguises with his lips while harbouring deceit; 'believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart.' Flattery hides malice. Do not trust honeyed words from a false heart.

Burning lips over a wicked heart are like a clay pot coated with silver dross; the hater disguises with his lips while harbouring deceit; 'believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart.' Flattery hides malice. Do not trust honeyed words from a false heart.

Burning lips over a wicked heart are like a clay pot coated with silver dross; the hater disguises with his lips while harbouring deceit; 'believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart.' Flattery hides malice. Do not trust honeyed words from a false heart.

Though hatred is covered by deceit, its wickedness shall be shown openly; 'whoso diggeth a pit shall fall therein'; a lying tongue hates those it wounds, and a flattering mouth works ruin. Malice is exposed at last. The trap set for others snares its maker.
